titleStmt.title: 398. Memorandum From the President’s Deputy Special Assistant for National Security Affairs (Kaysen) to the Deputy Secretary of State () to the Deputy Secretary of State (Ball))

div.head: 398. Memorandum From the President’s Deputy Special Assistant for National Security Affairs (Kaysen) to the Deputy Secretary of State (Ball)Source: Kennedy Library, National Security Files, Congo. Secret.

list.item: While it is difficult to look ahead in the present chaos when we don’t really know what is happening from day to day, it is also necessary. It seems to me that the situation we must deal with in about two or three days will be as follows. Tshombe will be well dug in at Jadotville and Kolwezi with no intention of giving himself up. There may be some progress on the part of the UMHK in dealing with the Central Government, but it will be far from conclusive. Tshombe will be making conditions, and Adoula and the UN will be refusing to talk to him.
From our point of view, it is worth taking large risks in order to settle the situation in an acceptable manner in the next two weeks.

item.p: The best prospect I can see for doing this at the moment is to try for the next week to maintain heavy pressure on all parties. This will require us to emphasize different things to each of them. This in turn may require more control and security than is inherently compatible with the nature of the situation, but it still looks like our best bet. We would ask the UN to maintain the public position of last Monday offering Tshombe something like the guarantees contained in our instruction to Stevenson, at the same time requiring specific performance on a concrete list of items within two weeks. Privately we would try to convey to Tshombe through diplomatic and clandestine channels the proposition that if he did not negotiate, the UN would resume the war with all-out U.S. support. This calls for the continued flow of our equipment to the UN amidst continuing publicity. In New York we would also say privately that as long as the UN followed the proposed line, we would give them maximum support and, if necessary, direct military assistance required to succeed in whatever renewed fighting occurred. However, if they or Adoula definitely closed the door in Tshombe’s face, we would be forced to withdraw from the operation. In any event, we would make clear that the military phase must be liquidated before the end of the month.

In Léopoldville we would say something similar, emphasizing that this was Adoula’s last chance, that if Adoula and the people around him want to succeed in bringing off a deal, they would need to show flexibility on their part. We could not go on assisting the Central Government indefinitely, if war with Katanga continued; but if they succeeded in bringing about a deal on re-integration, we would be in a good position to provide economic and military assistance in the future.

In Brussels and London we would point out to the UMHK and the governments that this was essentially their last chance. If they didn’t join with the Central Government and use their influence on Tshombe to make him negotiate, we could not answer for the next step the UN would inevitably take; we were bound to support their fight by our commitments to the UN, and the result might well be a total loss for them.

list.item: I think if we followed this program intensively for a week, we could succeed. The gravest obstacle in the way of its successful execution is the lack of the people we need for the job in every place but Brussels. However, if we get Cleveland off to Léopoldville, perhaps then George McGhee directly to Elisabethville and handle U Thant by a visit from you early next week, we might just bring it off.

The alternative I see is further obduracy on all sides, followed by renewed fighting in two weeks or so, under circumstances guaranteeing the maximum of bloodshed and the minimum probability of success.

closer.signed: Carl Kaysen Printed from a copy that bears this typed signature.
